4.html: not referring, as Michaelis thinks, to the reformation begun the year before, that is, the twelfth of Josiah; it means--now at once, now at last. contrasted with the "stock" whom they had heretofore called on as "father" ( Jr 2:27 ; Lu 15:18 ). rather, "thou wast." that is, husband ( Jr 2:2 ; Pr 2:17 ; Ho 2:7 , 15 ). Husband and father are the two most endearing of ties.

10.html: Some take this verse of Judah, to whom the end of Jr 3:8 refers. But Jr 3:10 puts Judah in contrast to Israel in this verse. "Yet for all this," referring to the sad example of Israel; if Jr 3:9 referred to Judah, "she" would have been written in Jr 3:10 , not "Judah." Translate, "It (the putting away of Israel) had come to pass through ... whoredom; and (that is, for) she (Israel) had defiled the land" &c. [Maurer]. English Version, however, may be explained to refer to Israel. "infamy." [Ewald]. Maurer not so well takes it from the Hebrew root, "voice," "fame."

16.html: literally, "I am Lord," that is, husband to you (so Jr 31:32 ; compare Ho 2:19 , 20; Is 54:5 ). Gesenius, following the Septuagint version of Jr 31:32 , and Paul's quotation of it ( He 8:9 ), translates, "I have rejected you"; so the corresponding Arabic, and the idea of lordship, may pass into that of looking down upon, and so rejecting. But the Septuagint in this passage translates, "I will be Lord over you." And the "for" has much more force in English Version than in that of Gesenius. The Hebrew hardly admits the rendering though [Hengstenberg]. Though but one or two Israelites were in a (foreign) city, they shall not be forgotten; all shall be restored ( Am 9:9 ). So, in the spiritual Israel, God gathers one convert here, another there, into His Church; not the least one is lost ( Mt 18:14 ; Ro 11:5 ; compare Jr 24:5-7 ). a clan or tribe.

3.html: 19. The good land covenanted to Abraham is to be restored to his seed. But the question arises, How shall this be done? the Greek for adoption means, literally, "putting among the sons." that is, My children. "How shall I receive thee back into My family, after thou hast so long forsaken Me for idols?" The answer is, they would acknowledge Him as "Father," and no longer turn away from Him. God assumes the language of one wondering how so desperate apostates could be restored to His family and its privileges (compare Eze 37:3 ; Calvin makes it, How the race of Abraham can be propagated again, being as it were dead); yet as His purpose has decreed it so, He shows how it shall be effected, namely, they shall receive from Him the spirit of adoption to cry, "My Father" ( Joh 1:12 ; Ga 4:6 ). The elect are "children" already in God's purpose; this is the ground of the subsequent realization of this relationship ( Ep 1:5 ; He 2:13 ). ( Jr 11:5 ; Eze 20:6 ; Da 11:16 , Margin). a heritage the most goodly of all nations [Maurer]; or a "heritage possessed by powerful hosts" ( De 4:38 ; Am 2:9 ). The rendering "splendors," instead of "hosts," is opposed by the fact that the Hebrew for "splendor" is not found in the plural.

9.html: that is, the idols, whose worship only covers us with shame ( Jr 11:13 ; Ho 9:10 ). So far from bringing us "salvation," they have cost us our cattle and even our children, whom we have sacrificed to them.

15.html: 3. Transition to Judah. Supply mentally. All which (the foregoing declaration as to Israel) applies to Judah. that is, and especially the men of Jerusalem, as being the most prominent in Judea. that is, Repent of your idolatry, and so be prepared to serve the Lord in truth ( Ho 10:12 ; Mt 13:7 ). The unhumbled heart is like ground which may be improved, being let out to us for that purpose, but which is as yet fallow, overgrown with weeds, its natural product.

5.html: 8. Nothing is left to the Jews but to bewail their desperate condition. ( Is 9:12 , 17, 21).

11.html: continuing the metaphor in Jr 4:11 : 12 . Clouds of sand and dust accompany the simoom, and after rapid gyrations ascend like a pillar. ( De 28:49 ; Ha 1:8 ). The people are graphically presented before us, without it being formally so stated, bursting out in these exclamations.

17.html: 18. ( Jr 2:17 , 19; Psa 107:17 ). that is, the fruit of thy wickedness.
